serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw pecans is approximately 28g (about 19 pecan halves or one small handful), providing roughly 199 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Nuts, pecans — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170182)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw pecans used as primary nutrient anchor. HPB Singapore values for core macros (energy 711 kcal, protein 9.8g, fat 71.9g, carbohydrate 4.9g, fibre 8.4g, sodium 3mg, potassium 500mg, calcium 51mg, iron 2.4mg, cholesterol 0mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy and are preserved. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients sourced from USDA SR Legacy to supplement HPB data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pecans are not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 10 is estimated by analogy with other whole tree nuts (walnuts GI ~15, macadamia nuts GI ~10) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The very low carbohydrate content (4.9g/100g) and high fat and fibre content of raw pecans support a GI in the single-digit to low-teens range. Glycemic load per 28g serving = (10 × 4.9 × 0.28) / 100 ≈ 0.14, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3mg/100g), high potassium (500mg/100g), MUFA-dominant fat profile, high dietary fibre (8.4g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Raw pecans are strongly DASH-aligned due to their very low sodium and high potassium content; a 28–30g daily portion can support blood pressure management, but salted or flavoured pecan products must be avoided as they substantially increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (4.9g/100g), high dietary fibre (8.4g/100g), low estimated GI (~10), high MUFA content slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw pecans are highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management per ADA dietary guidelines; their negligible glycemic load, high fibre, and MUFA-dominant fat profile moderate postprandial glucose response, though portion size should be limited to ~28–30g per serving to manage caloric intake given their energy density of 711 kcal/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA (40.8g/100g oleic acid dominant) and PUFA (21.6g/100g), low saturated fat (6.2g/100g), zero cholesterol, high gamma-tocopherol (vitamin E), high dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of raw pecans in moderate portions (~28–30g/day) is consistent with AHA heart-healthy dietary patterns; their MUFA/PUFA-dominant fat profile, zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant capacity (gamma-tocopherol) support LDL reduction and endothelial health, while their caloric density warrants portion control to avoid excess energy intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA/PUFA ratio (~65.7g unsaturated fat vs 6.2g saturated fat), high gamma-tocopherol (vitamin E), high dietary fibre, high antioxidant capacity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw pecans qualify under the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out for whole nuts and provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit through their oleic acid, gamma-tocopherol, zinc, and magnesium content; consume raw or dry-roasted without added oils, salt, or sugar, and avoid processed or salted pecan products.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw pecans (Carya illinoinensis) are whole, unprocessed tree nuts native to North America, particularly the southern United States and Mexico. They are characterized by their light to medium brown color, a smooth, oval shell, and a rich, buttery kernel. When raw, pecans offer a firm yet slightly yielding texture and a distinctively sweet, buttery, and mildly earthy flavor, setting them apart from other tree nuts. They are versatile in culinary applications, commonly enjoyed as a snack, used in baking for items like pecan pie, pralines, and cookies, added to salads, oatmeal, and trail mixes, or incorporated into various savory dishes. Nutritionally, raw pecans are energy-dense, providing 711 kcal per 100g. Their macronutrient profile is fat-dominant, with 71.9g total fat per 100g, comprising approximately 40.8g monounsaturated fat (primarily oleic acid) and 21.6g polyunsaturated fat, alongside a relatively low 6.2g saturated fat. They contain 9.8g protein and a very low net carbohydrate content of 4.9g per 100g, complemented by 8.4g dietary fibre. Sodium content is negligible at 3mg/100g, while potassium is high at 500mg/100g, aligning them strongly with DASH dietary principles. Pecans are recognized for their high antioxidant capacity, being rich in gamma-tocopherol (a form of vitamin E), zinc, magnesium, and phosphorus. Their estimated glycemic index is approximately 10 (classified as low), making them a suitable food choice for Type 2 Diabetes management. Clinically, raw pecans contribute to cardiovascular health through their beneficial MUFA/PUFA profile, support blood pressure regulation via their potassium-to-sodium ratio, and offer anti-inflammatory benefits due to their vitamin E and unsaturated fat content. Due to their high caloric density, portion control is an important consideration for overall energy intake management.
common_user_queries: Can I eat pecans if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in raw pecans?
What is the GI of pecans?
How much fat is in pecans?
What nutrients are in raw pecans?
How many pecans in a serving?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170182), which is an approved primary source with an exact match for raw pecans. Saturated fat, fatty acid breakdown, and additional micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as pecans are not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which slightly reduces GI confidence but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong macro and micronutrient provenance.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; pecans are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. 2. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 and Vitamin D is 0.0 — naturally zero as pecans are a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. 3. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw pecans. 4. Saturated fat (6.2g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (40.8g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (21.6g/100g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170182) to supplement HPB data which did not report fatty acid breakdown. 5. GI of ~10 is an analogous estimate based on whole tree nuts (walnuts, macadamia) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); pecans are not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input dietary_tag 'wholegrain' has been removed as pecans are tree nuts, not grains; all other dietary tags (plant_based, low_sodium, high_fibre, high_fat_warning, keto) are retained as accurate. 7. No mandatory multilingual names are required for this food as it is a globally traded raw ingredient with no specific Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification requiring local-language naming under G6 rules. 8. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible best-match assignments; FoodEx2 code A00LQ is the standard EFSA code for pecans.

The description now offers a more comprehensive overview suitable for RAG/LLM training.
